explanations.1: The ectoderm is the outermost germ layer which differentiates into both the nervous system and the integumentary system (skin); the digestive and respiratory systems arise from the endoderm.
explanations.2: The mesencephalon (midbrain) is the only primary vesicle that does not divide further, while the prosencephalon and rhombencephalon each split into two secondary vesicles.
explanations.3: The diencephalon develops into the thalamus and hypothalamus, and also forms the eye cup which becomes the retina; the cerebrum develops from the telencephalon.
explanations.4: Neural crest cells, which are part of the neuroectoderm, migrate to form non-nervous structures including the craniofacial bones and cartilage; vertebral bone develops from the mesoderm.
explanations.5: The neural crest consists of cells that separate from the neural tube to form various components of the peripheral nervous system, whereas the neural tube itself becomes the central nervous system.
explanations.6: The frontal lobe contains the primary motor cortex and is responsible for planning and executing movements; the other lobes are primarily involved in sensory processing.
explanations.7: The hypothalamus is the primary region of the diencephalon responsible for regulating homeostatic functions through the autonomic and endocrine systems.
explanations.8: The pons serves as the major bridge between the rest of the brain and the cerebellum, containing the white matter tracts that carry the majority of cerebellar input.
explanations.9: The anterior (ventral) horn of the spinal cord contains the cell bodies of somatic motor neurons that innervate skeletal muscles; the posterior horn is dedicated to sensory processing.
explanations.10: Brodmann’s areas are a system of mapping the cerebral cortex into 52 distinct regions based on their microscopic cellular structure and functional specializations.
explanations.11: The internal carotid artery enters the cranium through the carotid canal to provide the primary blood supply to the brain; the jugular vein is responsible for drainage.
explanations.12: The dura mater is the tough, outermost meningeal layer that splits in specific locations to form the dural venous sinuses, which drain blood from the brain.
explanations.13: Ependymal cells are specialized glial cells that line the ventricles and, at the choroid plexus, filter blood to produce cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).
explanations.14: The third ventricle is the narrow, midline cavity located within the diencephalon, connecting the lateral ventricles of the cerebrum to the cerebral aqueduct.
explanations.15: A stroke, or cerebrovascular accident (CVA), is caused by a loss of blood flow to part of the brain due to either a blockage (ischemic) or a rupture (hemorrhagic) of a blood vessel.
explanations.16: Autonomic ganglia contain the cell bodies of motor neurons that regulate involuntary homeostatic mechanisms; sensory ganglia like the dorsal root ganglion contain only sensory neurons.
explanations.17: The trigeminal ganglion (also called the semilunar ganglion) contains the sensory neuron cell bodies for the trigeminal nerve, which provides cutaneous sensation to the face.
explanations.18: A fascicle is a bundle of individual axons within a peripheral nerve; a tract is the equivalent structure within the central nervous system.
explanations.19: The vagus nerve (CN X) is unique because it extends beyond the head and neck to provide autonomic innervation to the organs of the thoracic and abdominal cavities.
explanations.20: The gastric plexus is part of the enteric nervous system, which is capable of operating semi-autonomously from the rest of the peripheral and central nervous systems.
